title: The jumping coefficients of non-Q-Gorenstein multiplier ideals
speaker: Patrick Graf
abstract: De Fernex and Hacon associated a multiplier ideal sheaf to a pair $(X, \mathfrak a^c)$ consisting of a normal variety and a closed subscheme, which generalizes the usual notion where the canonical divisor $K_X$ is assumed to be Q-Cartier. I will discuss a recent work of mine on the jumping numbers associated to these multiplier ideals. The set of jumping numbers of a pair is unbounded, countable and satisfies a certain periodicity property.

proof_stages.label: Classical multiplier ideals and jumping numbers
proof_stages.summary: The talk recalls multiplier ideals for smooth or Q-Gorenstein varieties, Nadel vanishing, log canonical thresholds, and the definition of jumping numbers as the values where multiplier ideals strictly drop.

proof_stages.label: Non-Q-Gorenstein problem and stated discreteness results
proof_stages.summary: The speaker motivates De Fernex–Hacon multiplier ideals without a Q-Gorenstein assumption, states basic properties of their jumping numbers, formulates the rationality/discreteness question, reviews known cases, and announces new discreteness theorems.

proof_stages.label: De Fernex–Hacon definition via limiting pullbacks
proof_stages.summary: To define multiplier ideals when K_X is not Q-Cartier, the construction introduces the natural pullback of Weil divisors, m-limiting relative canonical divisors, approximate ideals J_m, and then defines the multiplier ideal as the unique maximal member of the family.

proof_stages.label: Reduction to Q-Gorenstein pairs using compatible boundaries
proof_stages.summary: For a chosen m realizing the maximal ideal, an m-compatible effective boundary Δ makes K_X+Δ Q-Cartier and identifies the non-Q-Gorenstein multiplier ideal with the ordinary multiplier ideal of the pair (X,Δ).

proof_stages.label: Uniform global generation input
proof_stages.summary: A conjectural global-generation statement for divisorial sheaves, together with the speaker’s partial theorem near isolated non-Q-Cartier points, supplies the uniform ample divisor needed to build compatible boundaries with controlled positivity.

proof_stages.label: Contradiction strategy for discreteness
proof_stages.summary: Assuming an accumulation point of jumping numbers gives an increasing sequence t_k and a strict descending chain of multiplier ideals, so it suffices to find one ample twist making all these ideals globally generated, forcing stabilization after taking global sections.

proof_stages.label: Construction of uniformly controlled compatible boundaries
proof_stages.summary: Using the global-generation theorem, the proof constructs m-compatible boundaries Δ_m that are independent of m up to Q-linear equivalence, then applies Nadel vanishing and Castelnuovo–Mumford regularity to obtain global generation of the corresponding Q-Gorenstein multiplier ideals.

proof_stages.label: Identification with original ideals and conclusion
proof_stages.summary: For sufficiently divisible m the approximate, compatible-boundary, and original multiplier ideals agree for the sequence t_k, giving the desired uniform global generation and contradicting the strict infinite descent, hence proving discreteness in the stated case.

examples.name: Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing as the klt case
examples.note: The speaker notes that Nadel vanishing recovers Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing when the pair is klt, because the multiplier ideal is then trivial.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker derives Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing as the klt special case of Nadel vanishing by noting that for a klt pair the multiplier ideal is trivial, so the Nadel statement reduces to the usual vanishing

passes.reason: The klt specialization has trivial multiplier ideal O_X, so Nadel vanishing reduces to the ordinary Kawamata–Viehweg case rather than refuting anything.
the klt specialization has trivial multiplier ideal O_X, making it a degenerate special case of Nadel vanishing rather than an adversarial example

It is the klt specialization where the multiplier ideal is the trivial unit ideal, used only to recover Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing from Nadel vanishing.
The klt specialization has the trivial multiplier ideal O_X and is used only to recover Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing.
The klt specialization has the trivial multiplier ideal O_X and is used to illustrate Nadel vanishing as a generalization.
used as the trivial-multiplier-ideal klt special case of Nadel vanishing, not to refute a claim.

Used only as the standard klt special case of Nadel vanishing, not to refute a claim, and klt pairs/Kawamata–Viehweg vanishing are not structurally sporadic or extremal.
It is used only as the specialization of Nadel vanishing where the klt multiplier ideal is the trivial ideal O_X.

examples.name: Positive-characteristic test ideals
examples.note: Test ideals are presented as the characteristic-p analogue of multiplier ideals that does not require a Q-Gorenstein hypothesis, motivating the non-Q-Gorenstein characteristic-zero theory.
examples.engagement_reason: positive-characteristic test ideals are invoked only as motivation and analogy, with general facts stated about Frobenius definitions and reduction mod p but no concrete calculation or construction carried out

passes.reason: Test ideals are invoked as the positive-characteristic analogue motivating the non-Q-Gorenstein theory, with no structurally exceptional or adversarial role.
test ideals are standard positive-characteristic analogues used as motivation for dropping Q-Gorenstein hypotheses, not to refute a claim
Positive-characteristic test ideals are invoked as a standard analogue/motivation for multiplier ideals without a Q-Gorenstein assumption.
Test ideals are cited as a systematic positive-characteristic analogue motivating the non-Q-Gorenstein theory, not as a structurally special object or a refutation.
Test ideals are invoked as the standard positive-characteristic analogue motivating the non-Q-Gorenstein theory, not as a sporadic or limiting object.

positive-characteristic test ideals are invoked as a motivating analogue for non-Q-Gorenstein multiplier ideals, with no special intrinsic structure singled out.

examples.name: Stefanov’s cone over a smooth surface
examples.note: This example shows that rationality of non-Q-Gorenstein jumping numbers can fail, thereby justifying the subsequent focus on discreteness.
examples.engagement_reason: Stefanov’s cone over a smooth surface is cited as a known source of nonrational jumping numbers, with no details of the cone or computation of the jumping numbers worked through

passes.reason: Stefanov’s cone over a smooth surface is used to show rationality of jumping numbers fails in the non-Q-Gorenstein setting, while the cone itself is not a sporadic or extremal object.
Stefanov’s cone over a smooth surface is cited to disprove rationality by exhibiting nonrational jumping numbers, while cones form a systematic non-exceptional family
Stefanov’s cone examples are used to show rationality of jumping numbers fails, while cones over smooth surfaces form a systematic family rather than a structural exception.
Stefanov’s cone is a standard cone-family example used to show rationality of jumping numbers fails by producing nonrational ones.

Stefanov’s cone example is cited to show rationality of jumping numbers fails, while cones over smooth surfaces form a systematic family.
